findings.title: tx.origin Authentication Enables Phishing Attack on withdrawTreasury
findings.description: withdrawTreasury() uses tx.origin for authorization instead of msg.sender. tx.origin always refers to the original EOA that initiated the transaction, not the immediate caller. The companion PhishingAttacker contract demonstrates exactly this exploit: if the legitimate owner is tricked into calling PhishingAttacker.claimReward(), that contract calls target.withdrawTreasury() with tx.origin still equal to the owner's address, bypassing the check.
findings.message: An attacker who can trick the owner into calling any function on the attacker's contract can drain the entire treasury balance (address(target).balance is passed as the amount in the PoC). All ETH held in the contract can be stolen.
findings.recommendation: Replace tx.origin with msg.sender: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); Never use tx.origin for authorization. tx.origin should only be used to distinguish EOA from contract callers in very specific cases, never as a security check.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Anyone Can Add Themselves as Admin
findings.description: The addAdmin() function has no authorization check whatsoever. Any account can call it to grant admin privileges to any address, including themselves. Combined with the destroy() function which only requires admin status, any attacker can become an admin and immediately selfdestruct the contract.
findings.message: Any attacker can grant themselves admin rights and then call destroy() to selfdestruct the contract, sending all contract ETH to themselves. This results in total loss of all deposited user funds.
findings.recommendation: Add strict access control: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); Only the owner should be able to add admins. Consider also requiring that destroy() only be callable by the owner, not just any admin.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls addAdmin(attackerAddress) — no restriction prevents this.
2. admins[attackerAddress] is now true.
3. Attacker calls destroy() — require(admins[msg.sender]) passes.
4. Contract selfdestructs, all ETH (user deposits + treasury) sent to attacker.
5. All user balances are now unrecoverable (mappings exist but contract is gone).

findings.title: selfdestruct Accessible by Any Self-Appointed Admin
findings.description: The destroy() function uses selfdestruct and only checks that msg.sender is in the admins mapping. Since addAdmin() is completely unprotected (Vulnerability above), any attacker can first add themselves as admin and then call destroy(). Even if addAdmin were fixed, a compromised admin could still destroy the contract unilaterally without owner approval.
findings.message: Complete destruction of the contract and theft of all ETH (user balances and treasury) held by the contract. All user funds are permanently lost.
findings.recommendation: At minimum, restrict destroy() to owner only: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'). Consider removing selfdestruct entirely as it is deprecated in recent EVM versions and its behavior is changing post-EIP-6049. If a kill switch is needed, implement a controlled withdrawal pattern instead.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.message: AccessControlFlawed.withdrawTreasury(uint256) (../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#35-43) sends eth to arbitrary user

[AccessControlFlawed.withdrawTreasury(uint256)](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L35-L43) sends eth to arbitrary user
	Dangerous calls:
	- [address(msg.sender).transfer(amount)](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L41)
findings.recommendation: Add access control modifier (`onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`) or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check. Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl.

findings.message: Uninitialized State Variables
findings.description: Solidity does initialize variables by default when you declare them, however it's good practice to explicitly declare an initial value. For example, if you transfer money to an address we must make sure that the address has been initialized.
findings.recommendation: Initialize all state variables in constructor
exploit_scenario: 1. Upgradeable contract's initialize() function lacks the initializer modifier.
2. The implementation contract is deployed without calling initialize() (normal for proxy patterns).
3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.
4. Since the implementation is the logic target of a delegatecall-based proxy, attacker now controls admin functions.
5. Attacker calls selfdestruct on the implementation (if present), bricking the proxy permanently.
6. Alternatively, attacker upgrades to a malicious implementation and drains all proxy storage.

findings.message: Sending native Eth is not protected from these functions.
findings.description: Introduce checks for `msg.sender` in the function
findings.recommendation: Add access control modifier (`onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`) or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check. Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl.

5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.

findings.title: Anyone Can Pause or Unpause the Contract
findings.description: The togglePause() function has no access control. Any account can call it to pause or unpause the contract at any time. This means any attacker can halt deposits and withdrawals for all users (DoS), or unpause the contract when the owner has intentionally paused it in response to an incident.
findings.message:
1. Griefing/DoS: Attacker can permanently keep the contract paused (calling togglePause every time someone unpauses it) preventing all deposits and user withdrawals, locking user funds indefinitely.
2. Security bypass: If owner pauses contract due to a detected exploit, attacker can immediately unpause it to continue the exploit.
findings.recommendation: Add access control: require(msg.sender == owner || admins[msg.sender], 'Not authorized'); Consider separate pause/unpause functions with appropriate access control rather than a toggle (toggles are harder to reason about in multi-admin scenarios).
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker monitors mempool for any togglePause() by owner that would unpause.
2. Attacker front-runs or immediately follows with their own togglePause() to re-pause.
3. Users cannot withdraw funds (userWithdraw requires !paused).
4. Alternatively: contract is paused for emergency, attacker calls togglePause() to unpause and proceeds to exploit.

findings.title: Treasury Balance Accounting Mismatch: Contract ETH vs treasuryBalance
findings.description: The contract maintains two separate accounting systems: userBalances (which tracks full deposit amounts including the 10% fee portion) and treasuryBalance (which tracks only the 10% fee). However, withdrawTreasury checks amount <= treasuryBalance but sends ETH via payable(msg.sender).transfer(amount). Meanwhile userWithdraw sends ETH equal to full userBalances amounts. The invariant 'sum(userBalances) + treasuryBalance <= address(this).balance' should hold, but: when a user deposits 100 ETH, userBalances[user] += 100 and treasuryBalance += 10, yet only 100 ETH entered the contract. This means sum(userBalances) + treasuryBalance = 110 > 100 = contract.balance. Users who withdraw their full balance will deplete ETH that was supposed to be in the treasury.
findings.message: The last users to withdraw will find insufficient ETH in the contract. If treasuryBalance is withdrawn by the owner first, user withdrawals will fail. The 10% fee is double-counted — it exists in both userBalances and treasuryBalance, meaning the contract is insolvent from the first deposit.
findings.recommendation: Fix the accounting: either reduce userBalances by the fee amount on deposit (userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value * 90 / 100) so users can only withdraw what they're owed after fees, or track treasury separately without double-counting. Correct deposit logic: uint256 fee = msg.value / 10; userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value - fee; treasuryBalance += fee;
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User deposits 100 ETH: userBalances[user] = 100, treasuryBalance = 10, contract.balance = 100.
2. Owner calls withdrawTreasury(10): treasuryBalance = 0, contract.balance = 90.
3. User calls userWithdraw(100): requires userBalances[user] >= 100 (passes), tries to transfer 100 ETH.
4. Contract only has 90 ETH — transfer fails (insufficient balance).
5. Alternatively without treasury withdrawal: if 10 users each deposit 100 ETH and withdraw 100 ETH, contract is drained and treasuryBalance of 100 ETH is fictitious.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [PhishingAttacker](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L105-L121) has payable functions:
	 - [PhishingAttacker.receive()](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L120)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).
findings.description: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).
findings.recommendation: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).

2. The implementation contract is deployed without calling initialize() (normal for proxy patterns).
3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.
4. Since the implementation is the logic target of a delegatecall-based proxy, attacker now controls admin functions.
5. Attacker calls selfdestruct on the implementation (if present), bricking the proxy permanently.
6. Alternatively, attacker upgrades to a malicious implementation and drains all proxy storage.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.description: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: AccessControlFlawed.transferOwnership(address).newOwner (../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#75) lacks a zero-check on :
findings.description:
[AccessControlFlawed.transferOwnership(address).newOwner](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L75) lacks a zero-check on :
		- [owner = newOwner](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L78)
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the vulnerability

findings.recommendation: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.

findings.description: Introduce checks for `msg.sender` in the function
findings.recommendation: Add access control modifier (`onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`) or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check. Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Anyone Can Pause or Unpause the Contract
findings.description: The togglePause() function has no access control. Any account can call it to pause or unpause the contract at any time. This means any attacker can halt deposits and withdrawals for all users (DoS), or unpause the contract when the owner has intentionally paused it in response to an incident.
findings.message:
1. Griefing/DoS: Attacker can permanently keep the contract paused (calling togglePause every time someone unpauses it) preventing all deposits and user withdrawals, locking user funds indefinitely.
2. Security bypass: If owner pauses contract due to a detected exploit, attacker can immediately unpause it to continue the exploit.
findings.recommendation: Add access control: require(msg.sender == owner || admins[msg.sender], 'Not authorized'); Consider separate pause/unpause functions with appropriate access control rather than a toggle (toggles are harder to reason about in multi-admin scenarios).
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker monitors mempool for any togglePause() by owner that would unpause.
2. Attacker front-runs or immediately follows with their own togglePause() to re-pause.
3. Users cannot withdraw funds (userWithdraw requires !paused).
4. Alternatively: contract is paused for emergency, attacker calls togglePause() to unpause and proceeds to exploit.

findings.title: Treasury Balance Accounting Mismatch: Contract ETH vs treasuryBalance
findings.description: The contract maintains two separate accounting systems: userBalances (which tracks full deposit amounts including the 10% fee portion) and treasuryBalance (which tracks only the 10% fee). However, withdrawTreasury checks amount <= treasuryBalance but sends ETH via payable(msg.sender).transfer(amount). Meanwhile userWithdraw sends ETH equal to full userBalances amounts. The invariant 'sum(userBalances) + treasuryBalance <= address(this).balance' should hold, but: when a user deposits 100 ETH, userBalances[user] += 100 and treasuryBalance += 10, yet only 100 ETH entered the contract. This means sum(userBalances) + treasuryBalance = 110 > 100 = contract.balance. Users who withdraw their full balance will deplete ETH that was supposed to be in the treasury.
findings.message: The last users to withdraw will find insufficient ETH in the contract. If treasuryBalance is withdrawn by the owner first, user withdrawals will fail. The 10% fee is double-counted — it exists in both userBalances and treasuryBalance, meaning the contract is insolvent from the first deposit.
findings.recommendation: Fix the accounting: either reduce userBalances by the fee amount on deposit (userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value * 90 / 100) so users can only withdraw what they're owed after fees, or track treasury separately without double-counting. Correct deposit logic: uint256 fee = msg.value / 10; userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value - fee; treasuryBalance += fee;
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User deposits 100 ETH: userBalances[user] = 100, treasuryBalance = 10, contract.balance = 100.
2. Owner calls withdrawTreasury(10): treasuryBalance = 0, contract.balance = 90.
3. User calls userWithdraw(100): requires userBalances[user] >= 100 (passes), tries to transfer 100 ETH.
4. Contract only has 90 ETH — transfer fails (insufficient balance).
5. Alternatively without treasury withdrawal: if 10 users each deposit 100 ETH and withdraw 100 ETH, contract is drained and treasuryBalance of 100 ETH is fictitious.

findings.message: tx.origin used for authentication — vulnerable to phishing via intermediate contracts. Use msg.sender instead.
findings.description: tx.origin used for authentication — vulnerable to phishing via intermediate contracts. Use msg.sender instead.

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [PhishingAttacker](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L105-L121) has payable functions:
	 - [PhishingAttacker.receive()](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L120)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).
findings.description: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).
findings.recommendation: Initializer function detected — verify it cannot be called more than once (use OpenZeppelin's initializer modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Upgradeable contract's initialize() function lacks the initializer modifier.
2. The implementation contract is deployed without calling initialize() (normal for proxy patterns).
3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.
4. Since the implementation is the logic target of a delegatecall-based proxy, attacker now controls admin functions.
5. Attacker calls selfdestruct on the implementation (if present), bricking the proxy permanently.
6. Alternatively, attacker upgrades to a malicious implementation and drains all proxy storage.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.description: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: AccessControlFlawed.transferOwnership(address).newOwner (../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#75) lacks a zero-check on :
findings.description:
[AccessControlFlawed.transferOwnership(address).newOwner](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L75) lacks a zero-check on :
		- [owner = newOwner](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L78)
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the vulnerability

findings.message: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.description: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
findings.recommendation: State-changing function without event emission — limits off-chain monitoring and indexing.
